I will check again to see if they check back, allowing me to see a free river card, or to see how they respond if they decide to bet now.

On the flop, I checked back bottom pair for pot control and deception. The turn 5h improves my hand to three of a kind (Trips), which is very strong and well-disguised given my previous passive action. The opponent checked to me again, likely holding a capped range containing Jx, 8x, or air. I must now bet to extract value from pairs that would check-call, building the pot while my hand is hidden. Checking back again risks losing value from hands that would call a turn bet but might fold or check-down on the river. A half-pot bet of 200 is a standard size to get called by worse made hands.

On the turn, the board has paired with fives. My opponent checked the flop, which often indicates they missed or are playing a marginal hand cautiously. However, their bet of 200 into 400 on the turn is a standard delayed continuation bet. While I have King-high, which could technically be ahead of some pure bluffs or lower high-card hands, I have no draw and no made hand on a board containing a Jack and an Eight. Calling here with just King-high and no potential to improve significantly (other than a King) is risky, as I would likely face another bet on the river and have no clear path to winning the pot without a bluff or a lucky catch. Therefore, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with T2o. This is a very weak starting hand, but I have a stack advantage and I am in position. Against a standard opponent, this is often a fold, but in a heads-up situation, I can occasionally mix in a raise or a fold. Given the 2-to-1 pot odds and the fact that I've been playing somewhat aggressively, I'll fold this hand to maintain my stack and wait for a better spot, as T2o realization of equity is poor.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with Q8o, which is an above-average hand in heads-up play (top ~43%). With effective stacks deep at ~93BB, this hand is a clear open. I have consistently been min-raising to 200 when entering pots from the button in previous hands (e.g., Hand #38, #34, #32). While the opponent ('Flash') defends the Big Blind frequently by calling, raising allows me to take the initiative and utilize my positional advantage post-flop. Folding is too tight for this hand strength, and raising is preferred over limping to maintain aggression and potentially take the pot down pre-flop.

Holding Ten-Deuce offsuit in the Big Blind, I'm facing a 2x raise from the Button. While the price to call is small (100 chips for a 300-chip pot), this hand is near the bottom of my range. It has no connectivity and poor post-flop playability, especially being out of position. I prefer to wait for better spots to commit chips rather than defending with the weakest part of my range.

I am dealt 2h 3d on the button. This is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. While aggressive button play is important in heads-up matches, 23o has very little potential post-flop and doesn't warrant a raise or a call here. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to exploit my opponent.

I am on the Button with QJo, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play (top 15-20%). My strategy is to open-raise to build the pot and utilize my positional advantage post-flop. A min-raise to 200 is the standard sizing to balance risk and reward, putting pressure on the Big Blind while allowing me to play a pot in position with a strong range.

I am holding 2h 4d in the Big Blind. The opponent has raised to 200 from the Button. While I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, 2-4 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in poker and plays very poorly out of position. Given the tournament structure and the goal of maximizing my stack over 150 hands, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better spot rather than defending with such a weak range.

I have T8o on the button, which is a standard opening hand in heads-up play. Historically, I have been raising to 300 from the button, but since the current status shows the pot is 150 and it is 50 to call, it means I am the small blind. I will raise to 300 to maintain my aggressive pre-flop strategy and put pressure on the big blind.

The opponent has raised to 300 (3bb), consistent with their previous opening size. While K6o has reasonable raw equity against a wide button opening range, the larger raise size worsens my pot odds (calling 200 to win 400, requiring 33% equity realization). Being out of position with a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand makes it difficult to realize enough equity to justify the call. I have previously folded hands like Q5o and T8s against this size. K6o falls into a similar category of marginal hands that are often better folded against a 3x raise to avoid difficult post-flop spots.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with J9o. In heads-up play, this is a respectable hand well within the standard opening range (top 50-60% of hands). My strategy has been to open-raise to 2bb (200) with playable hands to seize initiative and position. Folding is too passive for this hand strength, and raising is generally superior to limping to apply pressure. I will raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Seven of spades, a strong suited hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Pro, has raised to 200. While calling is an option to see a flop with good pot odds, I have noticed Pro has been folding to 3-bets in previous hands (like Hand 18). By raising to 600, I exert pressure, take the initiative, and can potentially win the pot immediately or build a larger pot with a hand that has significant equity and playability.

I am facing a 3-bet to 600 after opening with J9o on the button. The pot odds are 400 to call into a total pot of 1200 (33%), but J9o is a marginal hand that plays poorly against a 3-bet range. It is often dominated by better Jacks or Nines and lacks the connectivity of suited hands to realize equity well, even in position. Given that we are deep-stacked, calling with offsuit trashy broadways can lead to significant reverse implied odds. I previously folded 98o to a similar 3-bet in Hand #18. Standard strategy dictates folding the bottom of my opening range here.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with 9c 4h. This is a weak hand. Looking at my opponent's recent play, they have been folding to a lot of pre-flop raises from my button (Hand 37, 39, 41), but they have also been 3-betting or raising themselves. Given the stack depths and the nature of the hand, 94o is generally a fold in most GTO strategies from the small blind, but in a head-up match with these dynamics, I can occasionally limp or fold. Since I have 10,600 chips and want to maintain a steady advantage without over-committing on trash hands, I will simply fold this hand.

I am in the Small Blind with K5o. My opponent, Flash, has recently 3-bet my button raises three times (Hands 10, 18, 44), indicating an aggressive defense strategy against my frequent opens. K5o is a marginal hand that cannot profitably call a 3-bet and plays poorly post-flop if called. Given the opponent's tendency to re-raise, tightening my opening range by folding this weak offsuit King is the prudent adjustment to preserve chips.

I am on the button with 6d-7c, which is a marginal but playable hand in a heads-up tournament. Raising is usually the standard play here to maintain pressure, as 'Pro' has shown a willingness to fold to pre-flop aggression in previous hands (like Hands #1, #3, #6, etc.). A standard 3x open is effective here to build the pot while leaving room to maneuver post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 2c, a very weak unsuited hand. The opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3bb) from the Button. While I am getting pot odds of 2:1 (calling 200 to win 600 total), 62o has poor equity and terrible playability out of position. Against a 3x open, defending this hand is -EV. I will fold.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) in a heads-up match with King-Four offsuit. In heads-up play, K4o is a reasonable hand to open-raise due to the King blocker and position. It falls within a standard wide button opening range (typically top 70-80% of hands). A min-raise to 200 puts pressure on the Big Blind to defend while risking a small amount. If 3-bet, I can easily fold. If called, I will play the pot in position.

I am in the Big Blind with QTo. Pro has raised from the Button/Small Blind to 200. This is a standard heads-up opening range from Pro, and QTo is well ahead of his wide range and too strong to fold. I have 100 more to call into a pot that will be 400. While I could 3-bet, calling allows me to play a pot with a strong broadway hand and maintain pot control out of position. I'll call and see a flop.

The flop comes 4h Jh 4c. I have Queen-high with a gutter-shot possibility (backdoor) and two overcards to the pair of fours. My opponent, Pro, raised pre-flop. This board texture is generally better for the pre-flop caller (me) since I have more 4x in my range than a standard button raiser, although Jx is definitely in his range. Given I am out of position and have moderate showdown value/improvement potential, checking to the aggressor is the standard play here.

I have flopped trips (three Fours) with a good kicker (King) on a board of 4h-Jh-4c. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play, likely the best hand. The board texture is somewhat dry, though a flush draw is present. As the pre-flop raiser in position, I have the option to c-bet for value or check back to slow-play. Checking back here has several benefits: 1) It disguises the strength of my hand. 2) It protects my checking range (so I don't only check when weak). 3) It keeps the opponent's wide range in the hand, allowing them to potentially bluff the turn or catch a piece of the board (like a pair) that they would pay off later. Since I crush the deck, I am not overly concerned with giving a free card, although the flush draw is a minor risk. In Hand #38, I successfully checked back trips to induce action on the turn. I will adopt the same line here.

The flop went check-check on a 4-J-4 board, which suggests that Pro likely does not have a strong Jack or a 4. The turn 8c is relatively blank. While I could bet to try and take the pot now, my Queen-high still has some showdown value against his missed overcards (like Kx or lower). I will check to see how he reacts; if he checks back again, I may have the best hand or can see a free river. If he bets, I can re-evaluate based on the sizing, though I am likely folding to significant pressure.

I hold Trip Fours with a King kicker (Kd4s) on a board of 4h-Jh-4c-8c. After raising pre-flop and checking back the flop to disguise my strength, the turn brings a second flush draw (clubs). Flash checks again. While checking back the flop was good for deception, checking back the turn is too risky and loses value. There are now two flush draws on the board, and I want to charge those draws as well as get value from pairs like Jx or 8x. A delayed c-bet here is standard. A half-pot bet of 200 is small enough to get called by worse hands (pairs, draws) while building the pot for a likely river value bet. Checking again risks giving a free card to a flush or missing a street of value.

I have Queen-high (QTo) on a board of 4h-Jh-4c-8c. In the pre-flop action, Pro raised from the button and I called. On the flop, both of us checked. On the turn, Pro has made a delayed continuation bet of 200 into a 400 pot. While my hand has some showdown value against total air and I have two overcards that could potentially give me the lead on the river, I am out of position and Queen-high is a weak holding to call with on this board texture. Pro's range for a delayed C-bet includes many Jacks, mid-pairs (55-77), or even an 8, all of which I am currently losing to. Without a significant draw or a better piece of the board, I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.
9-8 offsuit is a standard opening hand from the button in heads-up play. I will maintain my consistent strategy of raising to 300 to apply pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Deuces (2c 2d). The opponent has raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button, a frequent play for them. While 22 is difficult to play out of position and often requires folding to post-flop aggression if a set isn't hit (as seen in my previous Hands #27 and #33), it is statistically too strong to fold pre-flop in a heads-up scenario against a wide opening range. The pot odds are 2:1, requiring ~33% equity, and 22 has ~50% equity against a random hand. I will call to set-mine, planning to fold if I miss the flop and face a continuation bet.

I hold Pocket Deuces (2c 2d) on a board of 5d 9c Ks. I am out of position in the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor. This board texture favors the opponent's range, which includes many Kings and higher broadway cards. My hand is currently an underpair to the entire board and has very low equity. In Hand #27, I held the same hand (2s 2d) on a similar board (8-T-K), checked, and folded to a continuation bet. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out serves no purpose as I fold out nothing better and get called by everything that beats me. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet.

I have middle pair (nines) on a relatively dry flop of K-9-5. While the King is an overcard, middle pair has significant showdown value in heads-up play. Pro has checked to me, which is standard. I will place a continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot. This sizing is consistent with my previous betting patterns and serves to extract value from weaker hands like a five or straight draws, while also providing protection against overcards like a Queen, Jack, or Ten. If I am raised, I can comfortably fold, but a bet here maintains my aggression and takes control of the pot.

I hold Pocket Deuces (2c 2d) on a flop of 5d 9c Ks. The opponent has bet 300 (half-pot) after my check. This board texture, featuring a King, favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. A bet of 1,200 (half the pot) is a standard value bet that is likely to get called by any Jack and potentially some Nines, given the pot odds.

I hold second pair (Nines) with a Ten kicker on a board of [4s 4d 9s Jc 3h]. My opponent has fired three barrels (Flop, Turn, River). The Turn Jack is a significant overcard that connects well with the Button's opening range (AJ, KJ, QJ, JT), downgrading my hand to second pair. Crucially, my Ten kicker blocks the most likely straight draw bluffs (QT, T8) that would have picked up equity on the turn and missed the river. Since I block the natural bluffs and lose to all Jx, overpairs, and 4x hands, and given the consistent value-sizing of the bets (half-pot on all streets), my hand is likely not good. Folding preserves my stack (approx 40bb) for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 8s 5h, a very weak offsuit hand. My stack is significantly smaller than my opponent's (4000 vs 15850). Given the hand strength (unconnected, low cards) and the risk of facing a 3-bet from an aggressive opponent who has re-raised me pre-flop recently, the Expected Value (EV) of playing this hand is negative. Conserving chips for a better opportunity is the correct strategic move.

I am holding 7s 3d in the Small Blind. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While I have a significant chip lead over Pro, there is no reason to play trash hands out of position or try to steal with a hand that has zero equity if called. Folding is the most disciplined play here to maintain my stack and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I am on the Button with T8o (Ten-Eight offsuit), holding 40 BBs (4000 chips). My opponent, Flash, has a massive chip lead (15850 chips) and has shown aggression by 3-betting my button opens recently (e.g., Hands #18, #52, #54). While T8o is a playable hand in heads-up poker, it is marginal. Reviewing my recent play, I have folded hands like K5o (Hand #46) and T4o (Hand #30) on the button. Since T8o is weaker than K5o and I want to avoid bleeding chips against an aggressive big stack in marginal spots, I will stick to a tighter opening range and fold this hand.

I am holding Deuce of Spades and Seven of Diamonds, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Although I have a significant chip lead and the opponent has been playing somewhat conservatively, there is no strategic reason to enter a pot with such a weak hand, even from the button. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to utilize my stack advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with 3c 5s, which is a very weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand (5-3 offsuit). With a stack depth of 40 BBs, there is no need to play trash hands. My opponent has a significant chip lead, and while I need to accumulate chips, playing the bottom of my range out of position post-flop (if called) or facing a 3-bet is not a profitable strategy. Folding is the standard decision for this hand strength.

I am on the button with a strong suited hand (Jack of Clubs and Eight of Clubs). Pro is currently sitting on a short stack of 3,900 chips (39 big blinds). My strategy throughout the match has been to apply pressure with frequent 3x raises from the button, and given that Pro has folded to many pre-flop opens, I will continue this trend. Raising to 300 total is a standard opening that forces Pro to either commit a portion of their stack to see a flop or fold.

I am in the Big Blind with A7o, facing a standard open-raise to 300 from the Button (Flash). A7o is a strong hand heads-up, well ahead of a wide button opening range (which can include any two broadway cards, suited connectors, and many weak offsuit hands). I have excellent pot odds to call (calling 200 to win a pot of 400). While a 3-bet is possible to apply pressure, calling is the standard low-variance play to keep the opponent's range wide and play a pot with a decent hand, despite being out of position. I will call.

I hold A7o on a Q-T-5 rainbow flop. This board texture interacts well with the opponent's button raising range, which contains many broadway hands. As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking is the standard play to defer to the aggressor. I have Ace-high showdown value but no pair or significant draw, so I will check and evaluate the opponent's action. Leading out (donk betting) is not advisable as it builds a pot with a marginal hand out of position.

I have a gutshot straight draw and a backdoor flush draw on a board that contains two high cards (Queen and Ten) which fit my perceived pre-flop raising range. Pro has checked to me, showing weakness. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to maintain aggression and attempt to take the pot down now. This sizing is efficient against a short-stacked opponent who has shown a pattern of folding to post-flop pressure.

On a board of [5c Qh Ts], the texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many Queens, Tens, and straight draws (KJ, J9). My hand, A7o, has only Ace-high showdown value and no significant draws (no straight or flush draws). Facing a half-pot continuation bet (300 into 600) out of position, I lack sufficient equity or playability to continue profitably. Floating with just Ace-high on this coordinated board is -EV. Preserving my stack of ~31BB is prioritised over defending a marginal hand in a disadvantageous spot.

I am on the Button with A2o (Ace of Diamonds, Two of Hearts) and 36.5 big blinds. In heads-up poker, any Ace is a strong starting hand and is well within the standard opening range. Raising allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the blinds immediately, and play the pot in position if called. My opponent has a significant chip lead, so I cannot afford to pass up profitable opening opportunities. I will make a standard min-raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. The opponent has raised to 200, making it 100 more to call. I have a significant chip lead (16,200 vs 3,500). King-high hands have decent equity in heads-up play, and the price to call (2-to-1) is attractive. I will call to see a flop and utilize my stack advantage to navigate the post-flop play, as I have been playing aggressively throughout the tournament.

I've missed this dry flop completely with King-high. On a board of 9-6-2 rainbow, there are very few natural draws and it doesn't favor my calling range significantly more than the opener's range. I will check to the aggressor to evaluate their continuation bet. Given my massive chip lead, I don't need to force a play on this specific board without any equity.

I hold bottom pair (Deuces) with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a relatively dry, disconnected flop (9h 6c 2s). While I likely have the best hand right now, betting has limited benefits: worse hands (like King-high or random air) will likely fold, and better hands (like any 9, 6, or pocket pair > 22) will call or raise. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, disguise the strength of my hand, and induce my opponent to potentially bluff on later streets. This line also protects my checking range. I plan to call a bet on the turn or bet for value if checked to again.

I have turned bottom pair (threes) with a King kicker. After the flop (9-6-2 rainbow) was checked by both players, it is highly likely that the opponent does not have a Nine or a significant overpair. By betting 200 into the 400 pot, I am betting for thin value against hands like Ace-high or potential draws (like 7-8 or clubs) and protecting my hand from being outdrawn on the river. Given my substantial chip lead, this small bet allows me to dictate the action and potentially take down the pot immediately.

I hold bottom pair with the best kicker (Ace-Deuce) on a board of 9-6-2-3. After I checked back the flop, my opponent leads the turn for a half-pot bet (200 into 400). This line from the Big Blind often represents a 'stab' at the pot sensing weakness, a semi-bluff with a draw (like clubs or a straight draw), or a marginal made hand (like a pair of 3s or 6s). My hand is too strong to fold for this price, as I am getting 3:1 odds and my hand is a bluff-catcher that also beats some lower pairs or draws. Raising would likely only get called by better hands or fold out worse ones, so calling is the most prudent play to keep the opponent's range wide and realize my equity.

On this board of 9-6-2-3-2, my hand is two pair (Threes and Deuces) with a King kicker. On the turn, I bet for value and protection, and Pro called. The river Deuce pairs the board, which actually weakens my hand relative to any higher pair Pro might be holding (like a Six or a Nine), as their higher pair will now make a better two-pair than my Threes and Deuces. I still have showdown value against Ace-high and missed draws (like 4-5 or 7-8). Checking is the best option here to reach showdown cheaply or potentially induce a bluff from a missed draw, as betting is unlikely to be called by many worse hands.

I hold Trip Deuces with an Ace kicker (A2) on a board of [9h 6c 2s 3c 2d]. My hand is extremely strong and well-disguised due to my check-back on the flop and just calling the turn bet. The opponent's line—calling pre-flop, checking the flop, betting small on the turn (200 into 400), and checking the river—suggests a marginal made hand (like a pair of 9s, 6s, 3s, or a mid-pocket pair) or a busted draw. Since they checked the river, they likely do not have a full house or straight, or they would be betting for value. I miss significant value if I check back here. A half-pot bet of 400 is an optimal size: it is small enough to get called by top pair (9x) or stubborn pocket pairs (88, 77) that put me on a missed draw or Ace-high, but large enough to extract meaningful value. It is unlikely I will be raised, but if I am, I will have to re-evaluate based on the sizing, though folding trips is difficult.

I have bottom pair (Threes) on a board of 9-6-2-3-2. My hand is Threes and Deuces with a King kicker. This hand beats Ace-high and missed straight draws like 7-8 or 4-5. After checking the river, the opponent bet 400 into an 800 pot. This 1/2 pot bet could be a value bet with a marginal hand like a Six or a Nine that was played cautiously, or it could be a bluff attempting to push me off a weak showdown hand. Given that I only need to be right 25% of the time to break even (3:1 pot odds) and I have a massive stack advantage, I will call to catch potential bluffs and prevent the opponent from exploitatively betting when I check the river.

In a heads-up match as the chip leader, K5o is a strong enough hand to open from the button/small blind. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to apply pressure on the shorter stack and try to win the pot pre-flop or set up a post-flop advantage.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kh Kc), a premium starting hand. My opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3bb) from the Button. I am out of position in the Big Blind with a stack of 4,400 (44bb). My primary goal is to extract value and build a pot with my strong equity advantage, while also denying equity to the lower end of Flash's wide button-opening range. Calling would under-represent my hand and potentially allow me to trap, but it also allows my opponent to realize equity cheaply and puts me in a difficult spot if an Ace flops. Given the stack depth, a 3-bet is the most effective way to build the pot and set up a profitable stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) for post-flop play. A raise to 900 (3x the open) is a standard size that charges drawing hands and weaker pairs while keeping the opponent's calling range somewhat wide.

I opened for 300 with King-Five offsuit, and my opponent responded with a 3-bet to 900. With a stack of 3,600, Pro is committing a significant portion (25%) of their chips, suggesting a strong range. While I have the chip lead and position, King-Five offsuit is easily dominated by the types of hands that typically 3-bet in this spot (Ace-high, pairs, and superior Kings). I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to apply pressure without risking a double-up for my opponent on a marginal hand.

I am on the Button with [9h 5d], a very weak off-suit hand (9-high). My stack is roughly 47 BBs, while my opponent has a significant chip lead (151 BBs). Looking at the history, my opponent 'Flash' has been defending his Big Blind aggressively, often 3-betting my button opens (e.g., Hands #54, #44, #18). Opening with such a weak hand invites a 3-bet or a call that leaves me playing a poor hand out of position post-flop (if he calls) or burning chips (if I have to fold to a 3-bet). While playing a wide range in heads-up is standard, 95o is at the bottom of the range and is a standard fold, especially against an opponent showing resistance.

I am on the button with Ace-Deuce suited (Ah2h), which is a very strong starting hand in heads-up play. My opponent is in the big blind with a stack of 4650 (about 46 BB). I have been consistently raising to 300 from the button throughout the match. I will continue this strategy to exert pressure and build a pot with a hand that has significant equity and playability.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Sixes (6h 6d). The opponent (Flash) on the Button/Small Blind has raised to 300. My stack is 4650 (46.5 BB), while Flash covers me significantly. Pocket Sixes is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, but it can be difficult to maneuver post-flop out of position if overcards appear. A 3-bet shove is too large (46 BB) and risks too much for the small pot, while a smaller 3-bet bloats the pot with a hand that often becomes a bluff-catcher or set-miner. Calling is the most prudent option to see a flop relatively cheaply, keeping the pot manageable. I am getting decent odds to call the additional 200 chips.

I hold a pair of Sixes on a highly coordinated 5h-8h-9h monotone board. I have the 6h, giving me a flush draw (to a low flush) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7). Notably, the 7h would give me a straight flush. Despite the strong draws, my current made hand is weak (3rd pair), and I am out of position. Leading out on this texture is generally suboptimal as it folds out bluffs I beat and isolates me against better hands or higher flush draws. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor, intending to evaluate their action. If they bet, I have sufficient equity to call; if they check, I get a free card to try and hit my straight or flush.

I have flopped the nut flush with Ah2h on a 5h8h9h board. This is the best possible hand at this moment. My opponent checked to me, and I want to start building the pot to eventually target their remaining stack. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is consistent with my previous betting patterns and is likely to be called by a wide range of hands including any heart, any pair, or various straight draws.

I am holding a pair of Sixes (6h 6d) on a 5h-8h-9h board. This gives me a pair, a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7), and a flush draw (holding the 6h). Specifically, the 7h would give me a straight flush. The opponent bet 300 into a pot of 600, offering me 3:1 pot odds (25% equity needed to call). Given my pair and strong draws (straight flush potential, straight draw, and flush draw), I have significantly more than 25% equity against a typical button c-bet range. Raising might isolate me against better hands (higher flushes, sets), while folding is too tight given my equity. Calling allows me to realize my equity and evaluate the turn card.

On the turn, the Jack of Diamonds (Jd) is an overcard to my pair and likely hits the opponent's range (AJ, KJ, QJ, JT). My hand strength is weak (4th pair), but I retain decent equity through my draws: a flush draw (6h), a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7), and specifically a straight flush draw (7h). Leading out here (donk betting) is ill-advised as it likely only gets action from better hands and folds out bluffs. Checking is the standard line out of position to the pre-flop aggressor. It allows me to potentially see a free river or evaluate a bet if the opponent continues. My goal is to realize my equity cheaply.

I currently hold the nut flush with Ace-Deuce of hearts on a board of 5h8h9hJd. My opponent has checked to me on the turn. Since I have the absolute best possible hand, my goal is to maximize value and build the pot. A bet of 600, which is half the size of the current pot, is a standard value bet that can be called by a wide range of hands including any pair, a single high heart, or straight draws, while setting up a potential shove or large value bet on the river.

I am facing a half-pot bet of 600 into a pot of 1200 on the turn. I hold a pair of Sixes, a flush draw (6h), and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) on a 5h-8h-9h-Jd board. The pot odds require me to have roughly 25% equity to call. Even if my pair is currently beaten by a Jack or an overpair, my combined outs for a flush (9 outs, though some dirty) and a straight (4 outs) give me sufficient equity to continue. The Jack is a good card for the opponent's range, but the price is too efficient to fold a hand with this much potential improvement. Raising is not viable as I have little fold equity against value hands on this wet board. Calling is the correct play to realize my equity.

I hold a pair of Sixes (6h 6d) on a board of 5h-8h-9h-Jd-3d. I missed my flush draw and gutshot straight draw on the river. The river card is a brick that does not improve my hand, leaving me with 4th pair, which is a very weak showdown hand. I am out of position against an opponent who has shown aggression by betting the flop and turn. Leading out (donk betting) serves no purpose as it folds out bluffs I beat and gets called or raised by better hands. My only path to winning this pot is if the opponent checks back a weaker hand (missed non-heart draw, A-high) or a hand with similar showdown value. Therefore, I will check and likely fold to a significant bet.

I hold the nut flush with Ace-Deuce of hearts on a board of 5h-8h-9h-Jd-3d. My opponent has checked to me on the river after calling 1/2 pot bets on both the flop and the turn. To maximize value, I will continue with my 1/2 pot betting sizing. A bet of 1200 into a 2400 pot is consistent with my previous action and is sized to be called by a wide range of hands that my opponent might hold, such as a lower flush, a straight, or even a strong two-pair/set, whereas an all-in shove might scare off everything but the absolute strongest hands.

I hold a pair of Sixes (6h 6d) on a board of 5h 8h 9h Jd 3d. While I had a flush draw and gutshot straight draw on the flop and turn, I have missed both on the river. I am left with a weak pair of Sixes, which is effectively 4th pair on this board. The opponent has fired three barrels (flop, turn, and river) on a very wet, monotone texture. On a monotone board, it is difficult for the opponent to have 'blocker bluffs' (like holding the Ace of Hearts) because holding a high heart constitutes a made flush. Therefore, their betting range is heavily weighted towards value hands (flushes, straights, sets, and strong top pairs like AJ). My hand beats only pure air bluffs, which are rare in this spot. Getting 3:1 pot odds is not sufficient to call with such a weak holding against a triple-barrel line. I must fold.

I hold T7o on the button with a 35BB stack. My opponent, Flash, has a massive chip lead (163BB) and has shown aggression with 3-bets in previous hands. Reviewing my recent button play (Hands 60, 62, 66), I have consistently folded similar marginal hands like T8o, T6o, and 97o to preserve chips and avoid marginal spots out of position or facing 3-bets. Maintaining consistency with this tight strategy to wait for better opportunities is prudent here.

Holding K5o on the button in a heads-up situation, this is a standard opening hand. My opponent has a relatively short stack (34 BB), and consistent pressure has been effective. I will raise to 3x the big blind, which is consistent with my previous opening sizes, to either take the pot immediately or see a flop with a king-high hand that holds decent equity.

The opponent raises to 300 (3BB). I hold 3s 5h in the Big Blind. The pot odds are calling 200 to win 400, requiring 33% equity to break even directly. 53o has only about 32% raw equity against a random hand and performs poorly against a standard raising range. Furthermore, being out of position post-flop significantly reduces my ability to realize that equity. While the opponent is aggressive, 53o is at the very bottom of my range and is a standard fold to a 3x open.

I hold Ace-King offsuit (Ad Kh) on the Button, which is a premium starting hand, especially in heads-up play. My stack is currently 33.5 big blinds (3350 chips), while my opponent is deep-stacked. My goal is to maximize value with this strong hand. Folding is not an option. Limping is passive and allows the Big Blind to see a free flop with a wide range, which is not ideal for AKo as it profits from fold equity or building a pot pre-flop. An open shove is too large (over 30bb) and would likely only get called by hands that are flipping or dominating me (like pairs), causing me to lose value from dominated Ax or Kx hands that would call a smaller raise. Therefore, a standard min-raise to 200 is the best play. It builds the pot, keeps the opponent's calling range wide, and gives me the opportunity to 4-bet shove if the aggressive opponent decides to 3-bet.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh 3h. Pro has raised to 2x (200). With 100 more to call into a pot of 300, I am getting 3:1 odds. Qh 3h is a suited hand that plays reasonably well post-flop, and I have a significant chip lead over Pro (16,500 vs 3,200). Calling allows me to see a flop cheaply and evaluate.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh 3h. I called a pre-flop raise. The flop is Th 2d 8s, which does not hit my range well, although I do have a backdoor heart flush draw and two overcards to the 8 and 2. Pro has not made a continuation bet yet. In this heads-up situation, checking to the aggressor is the standard play to see how they react to this relatively dry board.

I hold Ad Kh on a Th 2d 8s flop. I raised pre-flop and the Big Blind called. The board texture is somewhat dry but connects reasonably well with a typical Big Blind defense range (Tx, 8x, 97, J9, etc.). While I have two overcards and good showdown value with Ace-high, a continuation bet here is risky because I have little fold equity against top or middle pair, which are well within my opponent's range. Furthermore, if I bet and face a check-raise, I would likely have to fold a hand that still has significant equity. Checking back allows me to exercise pot control, realize my equity by seeing a free turn card, and potentially induce bluffs or call down later if I improve or if my opponent shows weakness. It is a standard line to check back AK on this type of board texture to avoid bloating the pot with just Ace-high.

The turn 9d adds more connectivity to the board (8-9-T). Pro checked back on the flop, which often indicates a weak hand, an Ace-high, or a marginal made hand like a small pair. My Queen-high has very little showdown value, but I do have two overcards to the middle of the board and a backdoor flush draw. I will check to see Pro's reaction. If Pro checks back again, I might have a chance to win at showdown or bluff the river depending on the card. If Pro bets, I will likely fold as this board hits their potential calling range (like J-T, 9-8, etc.) more than mine.

I hold AdKh on a board of Th-2d-8s-9d. The turn card [9d] is excellent for my hand, giving me the nut flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (any Jack makes a straight) to go along with my two overcards. I now have massive equity (approx. 18-19 outs). The opponent checked the flop and turn, signaling weakness or a trap. While a bet could extract value or fold out small pairs, it exposes me to a check-raise, which would be disastrous as it might force me to fold substantial equity or commit chips with just Ace-high. Checking back is the superior risk-adjusted play; it guarantees I realize my equity by seeing the river for free, keeps the pot manageable, and allows me to value bet if I hit my monster draw on the river. My Ace-high also retains some showdown value.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh 3h and the board is Th 2d 8s 9d Ah. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) checked back on both the flop and the turn, which suggests a range of marginal made hands, Ace-high hands that didn't want to bet, or complete air. The Ace on the river is a significant scare card that hits a lot of Pro's checking range (like Ax hands). My Queen-high has almost no showdown value, but betting here into a pot that has been checked twice is risky, especially since Pro might have just improved to top pair. Given my massive chip lead, I will check and see if Pro wants to check it down or bet. I am prepared to fold to a significant bet as I have no piece of this board.

On the river, the Ace of Hearts gives me Top Pair with a King kicker (TPTK). The action has been checked to me on the flop and turn, and the opponent has checked the river again. This passive line strongly suggests my opponent holds a weak hand, likely a middle pair (Tx, 9x, 8x) or a missed draw, rather than a monster like a straight or set, which would likely have built the pot earlier. While checking back guarantees a win against worse hands, it misses value from an opponent who has shown a tendency to call down with marginal holdings (e.g., Hand #32, Hand #70). Given the board texture (T-9-8-2-A), the Ace is a scare card, but a small value bet of roughly half the pot (200) is likely to induce a curiosity call from a Ten or Nine. A check-raise is unlikely given the passive play, but if it happens, I can easily fold. The goal is to extract thin value from a passive opponent.

Pro raised pre-flop and then checked both the flop and the turn, which often indicates an Ace-high hand or a marginal made hand looking for a cheap showdown. The Ace of Hearts on the river is a major scare card that likely improved those Ace-high hands to top pair. Pro's bet of 200 into a 400 pot is a standard value bet in this situation. My Queen-high has no showdown value and cannot beat even a basic bluff if that bluff involves a King. Given my massive chip lead, there is no reason to attempt a risky bluff or a hero call here. I will fold and move on to the next hand.

I am holding Ace-Ten offsuit on the button, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. My opponent is relatively short-stacked with 35 big blinds. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to maintain my aggressive image and put pressure on the big blind.

The opponent has raised to 3x the big blind (300 chips), which is a larger sizing than a standard min-raise. While 87o is a connected hand, it is offsuit and at the lower end of playability, especially out of position. I need 33% equity to call, and while raw equity against a wide button range is sufficient, realizing that equity out of position against an aggressive chip leader who frequently c-bets is difficult. I have been bleeding chips defending against these 3x raises recently. With a 35BB stack, I can afford to fold the bottom of my defending range and wait for hands with better high-card value or playability.

I hold K4s on the button with 34.5 BBs. This is a hand with good equity heads-up, capable of making top pairs and flushes. It is too strong to fold and strong enough to open for value and to steal the blinds. My opponent has been defending their big blind, but raising gives me the initiative and position post-flop. A standard min-raise to 200 is the best play to balance my range.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s6d. Pro has raised to 200 from the small blind. I need to call 100 more to see a flop. Given that Pro has a relatively short stack of 3300 compared to the blinds (33 BBs) and I have a significant chip lead overall, I am getting 3:1 odds on a call. However, 96o is a weak hand that does not play well post-flop, and Pro has been playing quite tight-aggressive. Since the cost to call is small, I could defend, but I prefer to keep the pressure on when I have better connectivity or high card strength. In this specific tournament context, I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c 6c, a strong suited connector. My opponent, Pro, has a relatively short stack of 35BB. Throughout the match, I have consistently raised to 300 when entering the pot from this position to apply pressure. Given the strength of my hand for post-flop play and the stack dynamics, I will continue with a standard raise to 300.

I hold A3s in the Big Blind against a Button open to 300 (3BB). My opponent has been opening very frequently (wide range). A3s is a strong hand heads-up, well above the calling threshold for equity. With a stack of 35BB, shoving is a bit excessive/high-variance, and 3-betting non-all-in out of position is tricky. Calling closes the action, gives me good pot odds (calling 200 to win 400), and allows me to realize equity with a hand that can make nut flushes and straights.

I am out of position with Ace-high (Ah 3h) on a very coordinated board (Tc 8h Jd). This board texture (Jack-Ten-Eight) interacts very favorably with the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many broadway hands (KQ, KJ, QJ, Q9, etc.) and middle pairs. My hand has very little equity right now, having missed the flop, though I do have the nut backdoor flush draw. Leading out (donk betting) would be unwise as I am likely behind and would likely just be building a pot I can't win. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If the opponent bets, I will likely have to fold given the lack of immediate draws or pair equity. If they check back, I can try to realize equity on the turn.

The flop of Tc-8h-Jd is highly coordinated and likely hits many hands in the Big Blind's range. However, as the pre-flop aggressor with a massive chip lead, I will continue with a standard continuation bet of 300. This maintains my aggressive table image and puts pressure on Pro, who may fold many marginal hands that missed this specific texture. I have a backdoor club draw for some minimal equity if the hand continues.

I hold Ah3h on a Tc 8h Jd flop. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, connecting with many broadway hands, pairs, and straight draws. I have missed the flop entirely, holding only Ace-high with a weak kicker, a backdoor flush draw, and a distant runner-runner straight possibility. My opponent's half-pot c-bet indicates strength or at least a range advantage. Given I am out of position with poor immediate equity (likely <20%) and poor playability on future streets, continuing is negative EV. I hold pocket Queens (Qs Qd) on a Jd 4d 7h flop. This is a very strong hand (overpair) on a board with flush and straight draws. I hold pocket Queens (Qs Qd) on a board of [Jd 4d 7h Jh]. The turn pairs the Jack, which is a card that interacts well with my opponent's button opening range.
